content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

SUPERSEDED
IN PART
by I-92-218

March 26, 1991

Judith Sproul Davis
NIELSEN, MERKSAMER, HODGSON,
PARRINELLO & MUELLER
770 L Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Requests for Informal Assistance
Our File Nos. I-91-114 and I-91-143

Dear Ms. Davis:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the new provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act") 1/ limiting gifts and honoraria. Since your questions
are general in nature and you have not named a specific elected
official on whose behalf you have requested this advice, we provide
the following general responses to your questions.2/ Further,
since both your letters requesting advice deal with the new gift
and honoraria limits of the Act, we are providing this single
response to your requests.

Please be aware that the issues raised in this letter present
significant policy questions which will be presented to the Commission for consideration in the near future. At that time the
Commission may consider the continuing appropriateness of the
existing regulations in light of the new ethics provisions.

QUESTIONS

1. If a person invites an elected state officer to make a
speech in California and provides transportation, meals and lodging
in connection with the event, is the provision of the
transportation, meals and lodging a "gift" as defined in the Act?

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

SUPPRESSED
IN PART
Pac [illegible]

File No. I-91-114 and I-91-143
Page 2

     2.  If a gift is made to the spouse of a public official and
the official exercises no discretion or control over the use of the
gift, has the official received a "gift" as defined in the Act?

     3.  If an official is given a ticket to an event and suggests
that the donor also give a ticket to someone else, would the gift
of this second ticket also be a gift to the official?

CONCLUSIONS

     1.  Payments to an elected state officer of intrastate travel
and reasonable accommodation in connection with a speech currently
are not considered payments, gifts or income under Regulation
18728(a) and Regulation 18623(a).  Therefore we are advising that
such payments are not disclosable or subject to the new gift or
honoraria limits of the Act, or the conflict-of-interest
provisions.  However both of these regulations will be presented to
the Commission for their review in the near future.

     2.  Gifts to a public official's spouse from which the
official enjoys no benefits and over which the official has no
discretion or control are not disclosable gifts to the official.

     3.  If an official is able to exercise control over the gift,
the gift is likely to be deemed a gift to the official.  If the
official merely suggests that the donor make a gift to another
person, the resulting gift would generally not be a gift to the
official simply by virtue of the suggestion.  However, whether the
gift comes under the control of the official is necessarily a
factual determination which must be made on a case-by-case basis.

DISCUSSION

     Effective January 1, 1991, the legislature enacted a series of
statutes that limit or ban certain gifts or honoraria made to
public officials.  The Act now provides:

     No local elected officeholder shall accept any
honoraria for any speech given, article published,
or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
gathering, or any gifts, from any single source,
which is in excess of one thousand dollars
($1,000), in any calendar year, except
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection therewith.

Section 89501.

     (a)  No elected state officer may accept an
honorarium.

     (b)  "Honorarium" means, except as provided in
subdivision (c), any payment made in consideration

File No. I-91-114 and I-91-143
Page 3

for any speech given, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
gathering.

* * *

(f) For purposes of this section, "elected
state officer" includes the Insurance Commissioner.

Section 89502.

However, while the new provisions of the Act modify the
existing provisions of the Act, they do not automatically repeal
or redefine all the existing regulations concerning gifts and
honoraria. Thus, for example, although the gift limits are new to
the Act, they are based on the existing definitions in the Act, as
interpreted through Commission regulations.

1. Intrastate Travel and Accommodations

This letter confirms that your letter dated February 21,
1991, accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you
on that date. Regulation 18728 (copy enclosed) provides:

(a) As used in this section, "honorarium"
means a payment for speaking at any event,
participating in a panel or seminar or engaging in
any similar activity. For purposes of this sec-
tion, free admission, food, beverages and similar
nominal benefits provided to a filer at an event at
which he or she speaks, participates in a panel or
seminar or performs a similar service, and
reimbursement or advance for actual intrastate
travel and for necessary accommodations provided
directly in connection with the event are not pay-
ments and need not be reported by the filer.

(Emphasis added.)3/

Travel and accommodations which are not considered "payments"
under Regulation 18728(a) are not disclosed and do not trigger
conflicts of interest. Staff is currently advising that such

__________________________
3/ Regulation 18623(a) (copy enclosed) provides a similar
exception. Regulation 18623(a) provides: Free admission, food,
beverages and similar nominal benefits provided to an elected
state officer, legislative official, agency official, or state
candidate at an event at which he or she makes a speech or
participates in a panel or seminar, and reimbursement or advance
for actual intrastate travel and for necessary accommodations
provided directly in connection with the event are not gifts or
income.

File No. I-91-114 and I-91-143
Page 4

travel and accommodations are also not "payments" for purposes of
the new gift and honoraria limits. We have interpreted the
language of the new provisions to be consistent with Regulation
18728. (Koppes Advice Letter, No. I-90-517, copy enclosed.)
Thus, where intrastate travel is provided in connection with a
speech or with participation in a panel or seminar consistent with
Regulation 18728, the travel need not be reported and is not
subject to the gift and honoraria limits, or the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act.

Similarly, under Regulation 18623(a) the provision of
intrastate travel and accommodations is not a gift or income.
Consequently, the provision of payments for intrastate travel and
accommodations within the strictures of Regulation 18623(a) would
not be disclosable and not subject to the gift or honoraria limits
of the Act, or the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions.

However, both of the regulations discussed above will be
presented to the Commission in the near future for consideration
of their continued viability in light of the new ethics
provisions.

2. Gifts to Other Family Members

This letter also confirms that your letter dated March 5,
1991, accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you
on February 27, 1991 concerning Regulation 18726.2 and gifts to a
member of a public official's family. Regulation 18726.2 (copy
enclosed) provides:

(a) Gifts given directly to members of an
official's immediate family are not gifts to the
official unless used or disposed of by the official
or given by the recipient member of the official's
immediate family to the official for disposition or
use at the official's discretion.

(b) Gifts delivered by mail or other written
communication are given directly to members of the
official's immediate family if the family members'
names or familial designations (such as "spouse")
appear in the address on the envelope or in the
communication tendering or offering the gift, and
the gift is intended for their use or enjoyment.

(c) A gift given to the official, but
designated for the official and spouse or family,
is a gift to the official if the official exercises
discretion and control over who will actually use
the gift.

(d) If the official enjoys direct benefit
from a single gift, as well as members of the

File No. I-91-114 and I-91-143
Page 5

official's family, the full value of the gift is
attributable to the official.

Thus gifts to a public official's spouse from which the
official enjoys no benefits and over which the official has no
discretion or control are not disclosable gifts subject to the
limits of the Act and do not trigger conflicts of interest.

3. Gifts to Others at An Official's Behest

In our conversations of February 28, 1991, and March 1, 1991,
we also discussed direction of gifts by officials to others. I
can only confirm the advice with a qualifier. In those
conversations you asked whether a public official's "suggestion"
to the donor that tickets to an event be given to other persons
would mean that the tickets would be treated as gifts to the
official if the donor in fact provided tickets to those persons.
I stated that generally they would not be treated as gifts to the
official. This letter confirms that general conclusion. However,
I also cautioned that the conclusion was necessarily based on the
facts of the particular situation and that application of the
advice must be considered on a case-by-case basis.

I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
     Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:plh

Enclosures
